---
title: "RL and Rollout Planning"
phase: thesis
audience: public
status: current
owner: jan
format: html
---

## RL and Rollout Planning for ARIA-NBV {#rl-rollout-planning-literature}

The reinforcement-learning literature in the current ARIA-NBV corpus is most useful when read as a sequence of increasingly strong planning commitments rather than as a mandate to start with continuous actor-critic control. VIN-NBV establishes the local utility target by learning to rank candidate views using oracle Relative Reconstruction Improvement (RRI), while GenNBV and Hestia show how learned policies can act in continuous five-degree-of-freedom spaces once a simulator, reward, and state representation are available [@VIN-NBV-frahm2025; @GenNBV-chen2024; @Hestia-lu2026]. The thesis roadmap therefore takes a conservative middle position: first evaluate bounded discrete rollouts with oracle RRI, then use those rollouts to decide whether a value function, offline RL method, or continuous policy is warranted.

Janner, Li, and Levine's Trajectory Transformer is the cleanest literature anchor for treating planning as sequence decoding rather than immediately learning a stationary policy [@TrajectoryTransformer-janner2021]. Their method models complete trajectories as sequences of states, actions, and rewards, then repurposes beam search as a planning algorithm. The relevant lesson for ARIA-NBV is not that a large Transformer should be trained immediately; it is that fixed-horizon rollout search can be a legitimate planning primitive in an offline setting. ARIA-NBV already has typed candidate sets, oracle rewards, and deterministic counterfactual geometry updates, so a bounded beam over candidate-view sequences is the lower-risk analogue of Trajectory Transformer decoding.

Kool, van Hoof, and Welling give the probabilistic counterpart to deterministic beam search through the Gumbel-Top-k trick and stochastic beam search [@GumbelTopK-kool2019]. Their result extends Gumbel-Max from one categorical draw to an ordered sample without replacement, and then applies the idea to factorized sequence models without enumerating the full sequence space. This is directly relevant to the planned `ArgTopK -> ArgTop1_1 -> ... -> ArgTop1_H` ladder: deterministic `top-k` rollouts should be the first diagnostic baseline, while Gumbel-Top-k offers a principled later way to sample diverse high-scoring candidate branches without duplicating near-identical trajectories.

Double Q-learning matters as a warning label for any future ARIA-NBV value head [@DoubleDQN-vanHasselt2015]. In standard Q-learning, the same noisy estimates are used both to select and evaluate the maximizing action, which can produce overoptimistic values. Double DQN decouples the selector and evaluator by selecting with the online network and evaluating with the target network. If ARIA-NBV later trains a masked discrete Q-function over candidate views, this decoupling should be considered part of the evidence gate, because candidate utility estimates will be sparse, stage-dependent, and biased by the offline candidate generator.

Implicit Q-Learning (IQL) is the most relevant offline-RL baseline if ARIA-NBV eventually learns from fixed rollout datasets rather than querying an online simulator [@IQL-kostrikov2021]. IQL avoids directly evaluating unseen actions during value learning by fitting an upper expectile value function over dataset actions, backing it up into a Q-function, and extracting the policy through advantage-weighted behavioral cloning. The connection to ARIA-NBV is the dataset-support constraint: early target-aware rollout data will cover only sampled candidate shells, so any learned policy must respect what the offline store actually contains instead of extrapolating to arbitrary poses.

Haarnoja and coauthors provide a separate reason to keep stochastic candidate selection in the roadmap [@DeepEnergyPolicies-haarnoja2017]. Their soft Q-learning framework represents maximum-entropy policies as energy-based distributions in which higher Q-values correspond to lower energies. SAC later turns the same maximum-entropy control principle into a practical off-policy actor-critic baseline for continuous control [@SAC-haarnoja2018]. For ARIA-NBV, this is not yet a thesis-core algorithm, but it clarifies why a softmax or energy-style policy over candidate scores can be useful after oracle rollouts are trusted: it can represent multiple plausible next views, support exploration among near-tied candidates, and bridge supervised RRI scoring with later actor-critic formulations.

PPO remains relevant mainly because GenNBV and Hestia use policy-gradient training in continuous NBV settings [@PPO-schulman2017; @GenNBV-chen2024; @Hestia-lu2026]. The takeaway for ARIA-NBV is conservative: PPO-style online control should not be the first thesis experiment because oracle RRI is expensive and counterfactual modalities are incomplete. It becomes a meaningful baseline only if a simulator-backed reward loop or close-greedy continuous-control abstraction is available.

Taken together, these papers support a staged thesis claim. The first claim should be about bounded non-myopic search over trusted candidate sets, not about learned continuous control. A later value or policy method becomes scientifically meaningful only after the oracle rollout baseline shows headroom over one-step greedy selection, the VIN scorer ranks held-out candidates well, selected model rollouts are oracle-evaluated, and Rerun inspection exposes representative successes and failures.
